lesson.title: Attention Mechanism: The Math Behind Transformers
lesson.description: Learn the fundamental math behind attention mechanisms from scratch with interactive visualizations. Understand Q, K, V matrices, dot-product attention, and softmax step by step.
lesson.sentenceExample: The model helps you upskill in tech

steps.title: Step 1: Tokenization
steps.body: Split the sentence into tokens. We use: "The model helps you upskill in tech" and map each token to labels Model[0]..Model[5].
steps.whatHappens: The input sentence is segmented into ordered tokens. Each token receives a stable position index and readable label.
steps.howGenerated: Tokens are parsed from the user sentence, cleaned, and indexed in order. The app uses these indexes for all downstream matrices and heatmaps.
steps.whyItMatters: If token boundaries are wrong, every downstream score is wrong. Attention only works well when token sequence and order are correct.
misconceptions: Tokenization is not always word-level in real LLMs.
Changing punctuation can change token count and attention patterns.
aiTutorPrompts: Show how this sentence would tokenize differently with subword tokenization.
Explain why token boundaries influence attention quality.

steps.title: Step 2: Embedding Lookup
steps.body: Each token becomes a dense vector. Think of each dimension as a learned feature: meaning, role, and context clues.
steps.whatHappens: Each token index maps to a dense vector in embedding space.
steps.howGenerated: The app deterministically creates embedding vectors per token so learners can see stable, reproducible behavior while editing text.
steps.whyItMatters: Embeddings replace sparse one-hot vectors with compact dense vectors that are efficient and semantically useful.
misconceptions: Embedding dimensions are not directly human-readable features.
Similar vectors mean statistical similarity, not perfect semantic identity.
aiTutorPrompts: Explain static versus contextual embeddings.
Why are dense vectors better than one-hot vectors?

steps.title: Step 3: Create Q, K, V
steps.body: Project embeddings into Query, Key, and Value spaces using learned matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V.
steps.whatHappens: Embeddings are linearly projected into Query, Key, and Value matrices.
steps.howGenerated: For each token matrix X, the app computes Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, and V = XW_V with deterministic projection matrices.
steps.whyItMatters: Q asks what information is needed, K represents what each token offers, and V carries the content to aggregate.
misconceptions: Q K V are not separate token sets; they are different views of the same tokens.
These matrices are learned during training in real models.
aiTutorPrompts: Give intuition for why we need three projections instead of one.
How does multi-head attention alter these projections?

steps.title: Step 4: Dot Product Scores
steps.body: Compare one query against all keys with dot products. Preloaded educational scores are 1.29, 0.81, 0.95, 1.11, 1.05, 1.02.
steps.whatHappens: Each query vector is compared against all key vectors using a dot product.
steps.howGenerated: The app computes score matrix S_raw = QK^T, then the selected query row is shown in detail.
steps.whyItMatters: Dot product similarity decides which tokens are relevant for each query token.
misconceptions: Large score means stronger relevance, not guaranteed causal influence.
Negative scores are valid and common before softmax.
aiTutorPrompts: How does cosine similarity differ from dot-product similarity here?
Why can two semantically related tokens still get low score?

steps.title: Step 5: Scale by sqrt(d)
steps.body: Stabilize gradients by dividing scores by sqrt(d). This prevents very peaky softmax at larger dimensions.
steps.whatHappens: Raw scores are divided by sqrt(d_k).
steps.howGenerated: Scaled matrix is computed as S = S_raw / sqrt(d_k) before softmax.
steps.whyItMatters: Without scaling, logits grow with dimension and softmax can saturate too early, harming training dynamics.
misconceptions: Scaling changes distribution sharpness, not just absolute numbers.
Scaling is a standard part of transformer attention.
aiTutorPrompts: Show what happens to softmax if scaling is removed.
Relate scaling to gradient health during training.

steps.title: Step 6: Softmax Weights
steps.body: Convert scores into probabilities. Preloaded percentages are 20.2%, 12.5%, 14.4%, 17.0%, 15.9%, 20.0%.
steps.whatHappens: Each row of scaled scores is normalized with softmax to produce attention weights that sum to 1.
steps.howGenerated: The app applies row-wise softmax to scaled scores, handling masked values as very negative logits.
steps.whyItMatters: Softmax converts arbitrary similarity scores into interpretable mixing coefficients for values.
misconceptions: Softmax does not mean certainty; it normalizes relative scores.
A token can attend to itself and others at the same time.
aiTutorPrompts: Why do attention rows sum to 1?
How does masking alter softmax output?

steps.title: Step 7: Weighted Sum of V
steps.body: Multiply each V vector by its attention weight and add. Reference output vector: [0.82, 0.73, 0.76, 0.74].
steps.whatHappens: Each query builds a contextual output by weighted combination of all value vectors.
steps.howGenerated: The app computes O = A V and surfaces the selected query output vector.
steps.whyItMatters: This is where the model actually gathers information from relevant tokens.
misconceptions: The output vector is not one token embedding copied from somewhere.
Weights act on V, not directly on original tokens.
aiTutorPrompts: Explain intuitively what information the output vector contains.
What happens if one attention weight dominates?

steps.title: Step 8: Attention Heatmap (6x6)
steps.body: Visualize who attends to whom with a 6x6 matrix. Rows = query token, columns = key token.
steps.whatHappens: Attention weight matrix is visualized as a heatmap where intensity reflects strength.
steps.howGenerated: The app uses dynamic N x N weights from the current sentence and selected controls.
steps.whyItMatters: Heatmaps provide an intuitive view of token interaction patterns and local/global focus.
formulas: A in R^(n x n), A(i,j) = alpha(i,j)
misconceptions: Attention is not a full explanation of model behavior by itself.
Bright cells indicate weighting, not guaranteed factual grounding.
aiTutorPrompts: Identify which tokens are globally attended most and why.
How would punctuation affect this heatmap?

steps.title: Step 9: Formula View
steps.body: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d)) * V. Q and K find matches, V carries content.
steps.whatHappens: The full attention pipeline is compacted into one matrix equation.
steps.howGenerated: The same equation drives all dynamic results shown in earlier steps.
steps.whyItMatters: A single compact expression helps learners connect all intermediate computations.
misconceptions: This formula applies per head in multi-head attention before head merge.
This is attention math, not the entire transformer stack.
aiTutorPrompts: Expand the compact formula into step-by-step operations.
Map each term to one visualization in this app.

steps.title: Step 10: Full Flow Animation
steps.body: Animate end-to-end path: tokenize -> embed -> QKV -> positional -> mask -> multi-head -> softmax -> output.
steps.whatHappens: An animation links all computation blocks into one forward pipeline.
steps.howGenerated: Each node corresponds to data derived from the current user sentence and control settings.
steps.whyItMatters: Learners can shift from isolated formulas to a full operational mental model.
formulas: x -> X -> Q K V -> scores -> softmax -> output
misconceptions: The flow is parallelizable in matrix form even if shown as sequential blocks.
Real transformers include residual and normalization around these blocks.
aiTutorPrompts: Where do residual connections and layer norm fit into this pipeline?
Show equivalent pseudocode for this flow.

steps.title: Step 11: Interpretation
steps.body: High scores indicate relevance. Attention is dynamic: the same token can attend differently depending on query context.
steps.whatHappens: Users inspect score and weight patterns to infer contextual dependencies.
steps.howGenerated: Interpretation is grounded in currently selected query token and computed matrices.
steps.whyItMatters: Interpretation turns numbers into linguistic intuition and debugging signals.
misconceptions: Attention does not always align with human syntax intuitions.
One head alone is not the full story in multi-head models.
aiTutorPrompts: Explain this row of attention weights in plain language.
How can interpretation differ across heads for the same token?

steps.title: Step 12: TL;DR
steps.body: Q and K find matches (who should attend to whom), V provides the content to gather. Stack this with multi-head blocks, and you get a transformer.
steps.whatHappens: The complete mechanism is summarized into an operational memory hook.
steps.howGenerated: Summary is tied to the same dynamic computations learners just explored.
steps.whyItMatters: A concise mental model improves retention and transfer to advanced topics.
formulas: match with QK, mix with V
misconceptions: Transformer quality depends on more than attention: MLP, residual, norm, training data, and scale.
aiTutorPrompts: Give me a one-minute intuition recap for Q K V.
What should I remember before learning multi-head attention?

steps.title: Step 13: Multi-Head Attention
steps.body: Run 8 heads in parallel, inspect per-head heatmaps, toggle heads on/off, then concatenate and project into one output.
steps.whatHappens: Multiple heads compute attention in parallel using different projections and then merge outputs.
steps.howGenerated: The app computes per-head maps from head-specific Q K V projections and combines active heads with output projection.
steps.whyItMatters: Different heads can capture different patterns such as locality, syntax, or long-range dependencies.
misconceptions: Heads are not guaranteed to be interpretable or unique.
More heads do not always imply better quality.
aiTutorPrompts: Compare two head heatmaps and explain likely roles.
How does head dropout affect robustness?

steps.title: Step 14: Positional Encoding
steps.body: Compare sinusoidal encoding versus RoPE. Move sliders to see how position updates Q dot K behavior and relative geometry.
steps.whatHappens: Position information is injected so attention can reason about order and relative distance.
steps.howGenerated: The app shows sinusoidal vectors and RoPE-based vector rotations as position and theta change.
steps.whyItMatters: Attention alone is permutation-invariant; positional information is required to model sequence order.
misconceptions: RoPE does not simply add vectors; it rotates vector pairs.
Position method choice can affect long-context behavior.
aiTutorPrompts: Why can RoPE help with relative position awareness?
Compare additive PE versus rotary PE mathematically.

steps.title: Step 15: Masking & Causal Attention
steps.body: Apply triangular mask so future tokens become -inf before softmax. This reproduces decoder-only autoregressive behavior.
steps.whatHappens: Future positions are masked before softmax so token i cannot attend to tokens j greater than i.
steps.howGenerated: The app applies upper-triangular negative infinity masking to scaled scores and recomputes row-wise softmax.
steps.whyItMatters: Causal masking enforces left-to-right generation and prevents information leakage during next-token prediction.
misconceptions: Masking is applied before softmax, not after.
Encoder self-attention is often unmasked in encoder-decoder models.
aiTutorPrompts: Show how one masked row changes before and after softmax.
Why does negative infinity produce near-zero probability after softmax?

steps.title: Step 16: Efficiency Variants (GQA)
steps.body: Compare full multi-head KV cache vs grouped-query attention. Share KV heads by groups and visualize memory savings + block compute.
steps.whatHappens: Query heads are grouped so multiple query heads share fewer K V heads.
steps.howGenerated: The app computes KV-cache size estimates for full MHA versus GQA and visualizes tiled block-style compute intuition.
steps.whyItMatters: GQA reduces KV memory and bandwidth cost while preserving quality close to full multi-head in many settings.
misconceptions: GQA is not identical to MQA; MQA is the single-KV-head extreme.
Lower memory does not guarantee better output quality for every task.
aiTutorPrompts: Compare MHA GQA and MQA in one table.
How does group factor affect memory and quality tradeoffs?
